title: Podman container image /run & /tmp assumptions
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

Certain aspects of podman, in the context of the `upstream` flavors of the podman container image may assume `/run` and `/tmp` are a tmpfs.  This isn't possible to require from within the image itself, for every context (root vs rootless), so a `README.md` update is probably the most reasonable fix.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Follow `README.md` for running the upstream podman container image

2. Do something in podman (in the container) which assumes a `tmpfs`

**Describe the results you received:**

It breaks.

**Describe the results you expected:**

It works.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

I was requested to open this issue, I have no detailed knowledge/study of what's actually broken.

**Output of `podman version`:**

N/A

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

N/A

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

N/A

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md)**

N/A

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

N/A

comments.body:
@cevich Please correct me if i am wrong If i recall correctly this goes in parity with issues which @vrothberg faced while working with `pause` image. But underlying issues was `overlay` inside `overlay`. So wondering if this still an issue with any actual operation.

Could you also add the `Readme.md` link where this needs to be updated.
`/tmp` and `/run` are not needed to be on tmpfs, if the entire storage is transient.  e.g., when running in a nested container since the storage is destroyed once the parent container is removed
The problem is visible with pet containers, though. If the system restarts and the outer container is subsequently restarted we will run into issues.
(I think toolbox either mounts host `/tmp` or their own tmpfs to avoid this)
I think toolbox is mounting the host `/tmp` but also delegating podman to run on the host, not in the nested container, so it is the host responsibility to ensure `/tmp` and `/run` are on tmpfs

> Could you also add the Readme.md link where this needs to be updated.

IIRC, I was thinking of `./contrib/podmanimage/README.md` should maybe mention it so it doesn't catch users off-guard.
Where would you like it added?
"Sample Usage" maybe?  Though it's tricky enough of an issue, maybe it needs a dedicated section also.
You mean in the README.md file or TROUBLESHOOTING.md or podman.1.md?

Oh that's a thought, maybe all three?  I was thinking of the podman container images primarily because it seemed a likely place for someone to run into this.  But those other docs seem reasonable as well, especially `TROUBLESHOOTING.md`.
Care to open a PR against troubleshooting.md

TBH, I understand the basic technical issue here, but not the full practical effects or implications.  I don't feel I have enough experience to offer much on the troubleshooting topic.  I just thought we maybe needed to add a few `--tmpfs` args in the example...as a very blunt instrument to cover some abstract class of issues for people who use this container.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@TomSweeneyRedHat Care to take a stab at this?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I reread this and really am not sure what assumption about /run and /tmp being on a tmpfs?

Could you give me a concrete example of when this causes issue?  Are you talking about starting and stopping a container?

The issue is the same one we'd see on a host with Podman where our temporary files directory is not on a tmpfs, and an unclean shutdown occurs: the outer container is restarted, the temporary files directory is not on a tmpfs, the alive file is not removed as such; therefore, the state of all formerly-running containers that ran in the outer container is now incorrect.
Reminder: This issue was filed specifically against podman-in-a-container situations - where we most definitely cannot guarantee the type of `/tmp`.

I guess it would be nice if Containerfile had the concept of 

TMPFS /run

A Containerfile command isn't a guarantee, though that would perhaps solve the problem within `quay.io/containers/podman:latest` (stable) image.  In fact, can't we do that today with something like:

```dockerfile
RUN --mount=type=tmpfs,destination=/run
```

Or does that only affect the singular RUN command (layer)?  In that case, a new command (like TMPFS) which persisted mounts across all layers (including at runtime) would be a nice feature.  Though it would possibly break backwards compatibility with `Dockerfiles`, but maybe nobody cares.
Yes it would not work on Docker or Kubernetes.  I don't know if this was ever suggested in the past.
@nalind @vrothberg Thoughts.
Idea: Extending the `RUN --mount ...` with a new option, like `sticky=true` such that it persists for all subsequent layers and at runtime, maybe by adding a "magic" annotation?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@rhatdan @cevich @mheon this needs to be revisited to figure out what to do here.
Unilaterally extending Dockerfile syntax is a not on the table.  Is this a good use case for runlabels?
Runlabel seems like a reasonable solution to me.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Since we have a suggested fix, I am closing. Feel free to carry on discussion.
